topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon had been uneventful, a slow crawl through spreadsheets and lukewarm coffee. Then, leaning over the mailbox, a small, cream-colored card caught my eye. It was folded precisely, the edges crisp, and there was no address, no name—just a neat, looping script wishing someone a happy birthday. My fingers, usually steady when typing, trembled slightly as I pulled it out. Who had this? I turned it over, the paper smooth beneath my thumb, and a strange, hollow feeling settled in my chest. I looked around the quiet suburban street, half expecting a delivery person to materialize and explain the mystery. I tucked the card into my pocket, the paper feeling strangely significant, and walked inside, the silence of my apartment suddenly amplifying the quiet question hanging in the air.

[story 2]

I was midway through wrestling with a stubborn jar lid when the sound of the mail slot opening drew my attention. There, nestled amongst junk flyers and utility bills, was a card. Unsigned. It was bright turquoise, and the handwriting was elaborate, almost calligraphic, though I couldn't make out the message. I picked it up, my breath catching in my throat. I held it up to the light, scrutinizing the blank space where a sender’s name should have been. My brow furrowed deeply, my lips pressing into a thin line. I examined the envelope, searching for any clue—a stamp, a return address—but there was nothing. A sudden, sharp sense of being watched made me drop the card onto the kitchen counter. I stood there for a long moment, my gaze fixed on the card, my hands flexing at my sides, trying to reconcile the object in my hand with the reality of the ordinary mail delivery.

[story 3]

It was late evening, and the streetlights cast long, sickly yellow shadows. I retrieved the mail, a routine chore I usually performed without thought. But this time, something snagged my attention: a card, tucked into the bottom of a thick envelope. It was clearly a birthday greeting, the colors muted but cheerful. I unfolded it slowly, my movements becoming stiff and deliberate. My eyes scanned the surface, searching for any identifying mark, any signature that might unlock the context. Nothing. Just the well-wishes. A slow, almost painful tightening began in my jaw. I reread the message twice, the words swimming slightly. I felt a distinct pressure behind my eyes, a need to know where this object had originated, where this anonymous gesture had come from, and I couldn't seem to move away from the counter, rooted to the spot by the sheer lack of explanation.
